preFix.title: Hooked rug portrait panel
preFix.subtitle: A 30 × 40 cm hooked-rug panel worked from a simplified photographic source, in fine-cut wool strips on monks-cloth
preFix.excerpt: A portrait rug panel uses a simplified high-contrast design transferred to monks-cloth and worked in 4 to 5 mm wide fine-cut wool strips, hooking tonal areas separately in 4 to 6 tones from deepest shadow to highlight. The finished panel is blocked and framed, not used on the floor.
preFix.sourceNotes: Fine-cut portrait rug hooking is an established tradition in pictorial rug making, drawing on practices in North American and British craft guilds. Technique, tonal reduction approach, and design transfer process are described in general terms from widely available guild instruction; not from a specific copyright source.

content.text: A portrait hooked-rug panel takes a photograph or painted portrait source and reduces it to 4 to 6 tonal bands, each worked in a chosen colour using fine-cut wool strips hooked through
monks-cloth
backing. The finished piece is a
loop-pile
panel, 30 cm wide by 40 cm tall. It is
blocked
and framed as wall art, not used on the floor. The technique uses
fine-cut

strips of 4 to 5 mm width, cut from repurposed woven wool cloth or fine rug yarn. Each strip is pulled as a loop through the monks-cloth grid with a rug hook. This is an advanced project. It requires confident hooking technique, an understanding of
tonal reduction
, and the patience to work at small scale across a complex image.

items.name: Monks-cloth — 40 × 50 cm (includes 5 cm border on all sides for finishing)

items.qty: as required for coverage (approx. 200 to 300 g total for a 30 × 40 cm panel)
items.name: Woven wool cloth in 4 to 6 tonal values (deepest shadow through to highlight), cut into strips 4 to 5 mm wide by 15 to 20 cm long
Fine rug hook — size 8 or 9 standard hook, or a fine primitive hook
Rug frame — to mount and hold the monks-cloth taut while hooking
Permanent marker, waterproof — for transferring the design to the monks-cloth
Print of the source photograph at 30 × 40 cm scale, reduced to 4 to 6 tones in photo editing software
Rug binding tape — 2.5 cm wide cotton binding, for edge finishing
Foam blocking board and stainless pins — for blocking the finished panel before framing
Deep-rebate picture frame, 30 × 40 cm — to frame the finished panel

content.text: Method
Step 1: Tonal reduction of the source

Open the source photograph in photo editing software (Photoshop, GIMP, or similar). Convert to greyscale. Use the posterise function to reduce the image to 5 tones. Print at 30 × 40 cm. Identify the 5 tonal zones: zone 1 (deepest shadow), zone 2 (mid-shadow), zone 3 (mid-tone), zone 4 (mid-light), zone 5 (highlight). Assign one fabric colour to each zone. These can be five close values of a single hue (for a monochromatic portrait) or a combination of hues. An optional sixth tone can be added for a background or accent area.
Step 2: Transfer the design to monks-cloth

Place the printed tonal image on a lightbox or tape it to a window. Lay the monks-cloth over the print. Using a waterproof permanent marker, trace the outlines of each tonal zone directly onto the monks-cloth, working 5 cm in from the edge (the border margin). Mark each zone with its tone number (1 to 5) so there is no ambiguity when hooking begins. The traced lines will be covered by loops as you hook; they do not need to be fine.
Step 3: Mount on the frame

Mount the marked monks-cloth onto the rug frame so the working area is taut but not stretched. The grain of the monks-cloth should run straight on both axes. If it runs at a diagonal, the hooked loops will not lie in a consistent direction. Tighten the frame until the backing does not move when the rug hook is inserted.
Step 4: Prepare the wool strips

Cut wool strips 4 to 5 mm wide and 15 to 20 cm long from woven wool cloth. Cut on the straight grain. If using rug yarn rather than cut cloth, cut lengths of 20 cm. Keep each tonal colour in a separate container. The quantity required depends on hooking density: a fully filled 30 × 40 cm panel at one loop per monks-cloth hole requires approximately 200 to 300 g of fine-cut wool.
Step 5: Hooking the deepest shadow zone

Begin with the deepest shadow zone (zone 1). Hold the first strip beneath the monks-cloth backing with the non-dominant hand. Push the hook through the backing from above, catch the strip, and pull a loop of approximately 6 mm up through the hole. Move to the next hole and pull another loop. Continue across the zone. Adjacent loops should be pulled through adjacent holes or with one empty hole between them. The loops compress and support each other as the zone fills. Keep all loop heights consistent at 6 mm: too short and the loops will pull back through; too tall and the surface will look uneven.
Step 6: Working through the tonal zones

Work each tonal zone in order from darkest to lightest. Hooking darker tones first establishes the structure of the portrait before lighter, more diffuse areas are filled in. Within each zone, work the interior first and approach the boundary outlines last. Leaving the boundaries until the end lets you adjust the edge and refine the form as the neighbouring tones take shape. When beginning a new strip within a zone, bring the starting tail up through the first hole to the front surface and trim it to 6 mm; the same for the finishing tail at the end of each strip. Tails at the front are part of the pile and will not pull free if the surrounding loops are snug.
Step 7: Background

Once all tonal zones of the portrait are hooked, fill the background with a single colour that contrasts or complements the portrait palette. Work the background in rows parallel to the frame edges, hooking every hole or every second hole depending on the wool weight. The background is the largest single area and determines the overall visual character of the framed panel.
Finishing and binding
Blocking the panel

Remove the panel from the frame. Lay it face-down on a foam blocking board. Dampen the reverse side of the monks-cloth with a damp cloth or spray mister. Do not saturate the pile. Stretch the panel to the correct dimensions (30 × 40 cm) and pin it to the foam board with stainless pins at 3 cm intervals around the entire edge. Leave it to dry completely, which may take 12 to 24 hours. Once dry, remove the pins. The panel should hold its shape without rolling or distorting.
Binding the edge

Fold the 5 cm border of monks-cloth to the back of the panel and mitre the corners. Sew rug binding tape over the folded edge, stitching through the tape and the folded border. The binding should hold the panel flat and prevent the monks-cloth from fraying under the frame's backing board.
Framing

Use a deep-rebate picture frame (also called a canvas frame or box frame) with a rebate depth of at least 15 mm to accommodate the pile height. Do not use a standard flat-rebate frame: the loops will be compressed and flattened against the glass. Place the panel in the frame without glass so the textile surface is visible without a reflective barrier. Secure the panel to the backing board with a few stitches through the binding tape and the board, or use frame clips. Hang on a picture hook or wall-mounted cleat.

items.fix: Pull loops to at least 6 mm height. If strips are too wide for the monks-cloth holes, cut narrower strips at 3 mm and use a finer hook. Check that the monks-cloth grain is running straight, not at a diagonal.
items.cause: Loop height is too short (less than 5 mm), or the monks-cloth grid holes are too fine for the 4 to 5 mm strip width
items.problem: Loops are pulling back through the monks-cloth when the hook is removed

items.fix: Hook interior areas first, then define the edges when both sides of the boundary are nearly complete. A blunt darning needle can be used to lift and reposition individual loops at the boundary.
items.cause: Zone boundaries were hooked before the adjacent zone was filled, causing overlap
items.problem: The tonal zones look muddled — edges between zones are unclear

items.fix: Block the panel immediately after removal by dampening, stretching to dimensions, and pinning to the board. Most hooking distortion is correctable with wet blocking.
items.cause: The monks-cloth grain was not straight when mounting, or tension was applied unevenly
items.problem: The panel is distorted after removing from the frame

items.fix: Use a loop-height guide (a small piece of card cut to 6 mm) held against the hook as a reference. Loops that are visibly taller can be trimmed with sharp scissors held flat above the pile.
items.cause: Inconsistent loop-pull height between sessions or across workers
items.problem: The pile surface is uneven — some areas higher than others

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
